description: EU legislative push for Right to Repair, requiring manufacturers to make products repairable and provide spare parts. Public support grew from ~70% to ~85%+ across member states. Eventually adopted in April 2024. Tests consensus-building policy with broad popular support against industry resistance.

key_events.description: European Commission launches Right to Repair initiative as part of Circular Economy Action Plan. Eurobarometer shows 77% of EU citizens prefer repairing to replacing.
France implements pioneering Repairability Index (indice de réparabilité) requiring manufacturers to score products 0-10 on repairability. Other countries take notice.
Apple's self-service repair program launch (2022) seen as industry concession to regulatory pressure. iFixit partnership signals shift.
European Parliament votes overwhelmingly (509-3) in favor of Right to Repair directive. Cross-party consensus reflects public demand.
Council of the EU adopts directive (April 2024). Manufacturers must repair products even after warranty; spare parts available for 5-10 years.

calibration_notes: Tests gradual consensus building with steady support growth rather than dramatic swings. Unusual case where public opinion starts strong and only grows stronger, with minimal organized opposition. Key dynamics: environmental framing resonating across political spectrum, cost-of-living crisis making repair economics salient, industry initially opposing then capitulating (Apple), France as first-mover demonstrating feasibility, near-unanimous parliamentary support reflecting genuine popular consensus, and the rarity of a policy issue with 85%+ support in polarized era.
